subject: ap-government
title: Argument Essay — Expanded National Government Powers and Policy Making
stimulus:
The United States Constitution establishes a federal system of government. Under federalism, policy making is shared between national and state governments. Over time, the powers of the national government have increased relative to those of the state governments.

Develop an argument about whether the expanded powers of the national government benefits or hinders policy making.

parts.prompt: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning. (Use at least one piece of evidence from one of the following foundational documents: The Articles of Confederation, Brutus 1, The Federalist 10.)
rubric_criteria: 1 pt (Row A — Thesis/Claim): Responds to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning about whether expanded national government powers benefit or hinder policy making. Must go beyond restating the prompt and establish a line of reasoning.

Examples that earn the point: 'The expanded powers of the national government have made it more efficient, and if states had more power it would be dangerous.' OR 'The expanded powers of the national government benefit policy making because of the strength of the Constitution, the increase of cooperative federalism, and the advantages of fiscal federalism.'
parts.scoring_notes: Restating the prompt does not earn the point. The thesis must take a clear position and establish a line of reasoning. It may be located anywhere in the response.

parts.prompt: Support your claim or thesis with at least TWO pieces of accurate and relevant evidence. One piece of evidence must come from one of the foundational documents listed. A second piece of evidence can come from any other foundational document or from course concepts.
rubric_criteria: 0 pts: No accurate evidence provided, or evidence is not relevant to the topic.
1 pt (Row B — 1 point): Provides one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt (may or may not have a claim/thesis).
2 pts (Row B — 2 points): Uses one piece of specific and relevant evidence to support the claim or thesis. Must have earned Row A point.

3 pts (Row B — 3 points): Uses two pieces of specific and relevant evidence to support the claim or thesis, one of which must come from a foundational document listed in the prompt (Articles of Confederation, Brutus 1, or Federalist 10). Must have earned Row A point.

Example earning 2 points: 'The U.S. has already tried giving states more power, and it proved ineffective. The Articles of Confederation showed why a strong national government is better — with the Articles, the government could do very little and states had their own currency and taxes.'
Example earning 3 points: Above example PLUS citing Federalist 70 that a strong executive benefits the country because one person can make decisions and be held accountable.
parts.scoring_notes: To earn 2 or 3 points, the response must have a defensible claim/thesis (Row A). To earn 3 points, one piece of evidence must be from the listed foundational documents (Articles of Confederation, Brutus 1, or Federalist 10).

parts.prompt: Use reasoning to explain why your evidence supports your claim or thesis.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt (Row C — Reasoning): Explains how or why the evidence supports the claim or thesis — establishes the relationship between at least one piece of evidence and the argument.

Examples that earn the point: 'The Articles of Confederation made it harder to pass laws because each state has very different agendas, and it required over a majority to agree. To amend, it required unanimous decision. This makes it nearly impossible to add a new amendment to change policy.' OR 'This efficiency and accountability from a strong executive benefits policy making.'
parts.scoring_notes: To earn this point, the response must have a defensible claim/thesis (Row A) and at least one piece of specific and relevant evidence (at least 2 points in Row B). Simply listing evidence without explaining the connection to the thesis does not earn this point.

parts.prompt: Respond to an opposing or alternative perspective using refutation, concession, or rebuttal.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt (Row D — Alternate Perspective): Responds to an opposing or alternative perspective using refutation, concession, or rebuttal. Must both DESCRIBE an alternate perspective AND refute, concede, or rebut it.

Example that earns the point: 'Some people may argue that the federal government is too large, and that states are best suited to address the needs of their people without interference from the federal government. However, this is not true. The state governments are not equipped to handle all problems they face without the federal government. The federal government can act with uniformity to affect all states, ensuring everyone is guaranteed the same protections.'
parts.scoring_notes: To earn this point, the response must have a defensible claim/thesis (Row A). Restating the opposite of the thesis does not earn the point. Identifying an alternate perspective without refuting, conceding, or rebutting it does not earn the point. Refuting a foundational document rather than an alternate perspective does not earn the point.
